title: Elon Musk and Tim Cook Join Trump on Diplomatic Visit to China

contents:
Georgia Secretary of State Brad Raffensperger has emerged as a pivotal figure in the ongoing struggle over the Republican Party’s direction, embodying the tensions between the party’s establishment wing and its Trump-aligned base.
Raffensperger, who famously resisted pressure from former President Donald Trump to overturn Georgia’s 2020 election results, remains in office and is positioning himself as a key voice on election integrity — on his own terms. His survival and influence within the party, despite intense backlash from Trump supporters, is being watched closely as a bellwether for the GOP’s post-Trump evolution.
In recent weeks, Raffensperger has stepped up his national profile, appearing at GOP fundraisers and policy forums where he has urged the party to focus on future elections rather than relitigating the past. “We need to be a party that looks forward, not backward,” Raffensperger said at a recent gathering in Atlanta. “Voters want to know what we’re going to do tomorrow, not what happened yesterday.”
His message has resonated with some Republicans, particularly donors and suburban voters who grew weary of the party’s fixation on 2020. However, it has also drawn sharp criticism from Trump loyalists, who view Raffensperger as a traitor to the cause.
The Georgia Secretary of State’s office has also been at the center of ongoing election administration reforms, including new voter ID requirements and updates to the state’s voting machine system. Raffensperger has defended these changes as necessary for public confidence, while critics argue they amount to voter suppression.
Political analysts say Raffensperger’s trajectory will be one of the most closely watched storylines heading into the 2026 midterms. If he can maintain his position and influence, it could signal a shift away from Trump-dominated politics. If he is primaried and defeated, it would reinforce the former president’s continued grip on the party.
title: Georgia's Raffensperger Tests the Future of the GOP

contents:
Veteran Republican strategist Karl Rove has warned that aggressive GOP redistricting efforts in several key states could backfire, potentially costing the party House seats rather than protecting them.
In a recent op-ed for The Wall Street Journal, Rove argued that some of the newly drawn maps, while designed to create safe Republican districts, have inadvertently made surrounding districts more competitive for Democrats. “The law of unintended consequences is alive and well in redistricting,” Rove wrote.
The warning comes as courts in several states, including Ohio, North Carolina, and Georgia, have struck down Republican-drawn maps as unconstitutional partisan gerrymanders. The resulting court-ordered redraws have in some cases produced maps that are more favorable to Democrats than the original Republican plans.
Rove specifically pointed to Ohio, where a court-imposed map created three competitive districts that Democrats are now favored to win. “What was supposed to be a 10-5 Republican advantage could end up being 8-7 or even 7-8,” he noted.
Democrats have seized on the situation, framing the redistricting battles as evidence of Republican overreach. The Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee has launched a “Fair Maps” initiative targeting competitive districts in states where GOP maps were overturned.
The broader implication, according to election analysts, is that the Republican strategy of maximizing gerrymandered districts may have reached its limits. With courts increasingly willing to intervene and public opinion turning against extreme partisan redistricting, the GOP may need to rethink its approach to electoral mapmaking.
“The era of unlimited partisan gerrymandering may be coming to an end,” said Michael Li, a redistricting expert at the Brennan Center for Justice. “Both parties are going to have to compete on the merits rather than relying on map manipulation.”
title: Karl Rove Warns New GOP Election Maps Could Backfire on Republicans

contents:
Prime Minister Keir Starmer is facing the most serious challenge to his leadership yet, as a growing faction of Labour MPs openly calls for his resignation. The internal rebellion threatens to destabilise a government already grappling with a narrow parliamentary majority and a series of policy setbacks.
According to multiple reports from Westminster, at least two dozen Labour MPs have either publicly or privately indicated that they believe Starmer should step aside before the next general election. Discontent has been simmering for months, fueled by stagnant polling numbers, internal divisions over economic policy, and criticism of the government’s handling of public services.
The rebellion cuts across traditional Labour factions. Centrist MPs are frustrated with what they perceive as a lack of bold vision, while the party’s left wing accuses Starmer of abandoning core socialist principles. The situation has been exacerbated by a series of by-election losses, including in previously safe Labour seats.
“The party is sleepwalking into electoral disaster,” one senior Labour backbencher told The Guardian on condition of anonymity. “If we go into the next election with Starmer at the helm, we’re handing the keys to Downing Street back to the Conservatives.”
Downing Street has pushed back against the unrest, with Starmer’s allies dismissing the rebellion as the work of a “small but vocal minority.” A spokesperson for the Prime Minister said: “Sir Keir is focused on delivering for the British people. The party is united behind his leadership.”
However, the growing number of public defections suggests the unrest is more than just backbench grumbling. Political analysts note that the tipping point could come if senior cabinet figures begin to distance themselves from the Prime Minister.
The crisis comes at a particularly delicate time for the UK, with ongoing negotiations over post-Brexit trade arrangements, rising inflation, and growing public dissatisfaction with the government’s performance on the NHS and housing.
title: Labour MPs Call for Starmer's Resignation Amid Leadership Crisis
UK Government Announces Plans to Nationalise British Steel
